text: This case presentation from the Cleveland Clinic demonstrates single-part laparoscopic restorative proctocolectomy with ileal pouch anal anastomosis through the left lower quadrant ileostomy site in a three-stage fashion.
This thirty-nine-year-old female presented to the clinic with a medically refractory ulcerative colitis.
Since patient has a congenital atrophia In her right upper extremity, a right ileostomy site was deemed unmanageable for her and a decision was made to use the left lower quadrant for the ileostomy.
The surgeon performs a single incisional laparoscopic total colectomy, creating a left-sided ileostomy due to the patient's high steroid use and medically refractory ulcerative colitis.
The patient is positioned in the modified lithotomy position, which involves lying on their back with legs elevated and supported in stirrups, providing optimal access to the surgical site.
The surgeon excises a disc-shaped section of previously marked skin from the left lower quadrant and enters the abdomen using a muscle-splitting technique.
The surgeon introduces one 12mm and two 5mm trocars through the jet platform, preparing for laparoscopic access to the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon begins dissection from the right colon, identifying and ligating the ileocolic pedicle.
The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection of the colon, progressing through the tissue layers with visible separation of anatomical structures.
The surgeon identifies the duodenum and carefully preserves it during the procedure.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the ascending colon and performs a clockwise right to left total colectomy, visually detaching the colon from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon takes down the hepatocolic ligament and mobilizes the transverse colon by dividing the mesocolon and the greater omentum.
The surgeon ligates the left branch of the middle colic artery after taking down the splenic flexure, completing the dissection in this area.
The surgeon staples the rectosigmoid junction while leaving the inferior mesenteric artery intact, indicating the completion of this segment of the procedure.
The surgeon exteriorizes the colon through the ileostomy site, completing the process of bringing the colon outside the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon matures the ileostomy, leaving the rectal stem in the pelvis without implantation.
The patient's postoperative recovery was uneventful.
Six months after laparoscopic total colectomy, patient was taken to the operating room for the second stage procedure.

The surgeon performs a circumferential incision around the stoma site in the left lower quadrant, releasing the loop ileostomy. The ileostomy is temporarily sealed with a trio-viral stick and returned to the abdomen.
The surgeon achieves pneumoperitoneum using a single port access device, visibly inflating the abdominal cavity to facilitate further surgical access.
The surgeon ligates the inferior mesenteric artery at the level of the superior artery, securing the vessel with visible ligatures.
The surgeon performs dissection through the avascular plane posterior to the mesorectum, progressing down to the levator muscles.
The surgeon continues dissection on both sides after reaching the levator muscles, following the avascular plane posterior to the mesorectum.
The surgeon performs anterior mobilization, likely involving the separation and movement of anterior structures in the surgical field.
After achieving complete mobilization, the surgeon performs a digital rectal exam to visually confirm the level of dissection.
The surgeon uses a laparoscopic GIA-XVI stapler to apply two staple lines.
The surgeon creates a 15 to 20 centimeter ileal pouch using an ILA-100 stapler and TA-13 fires, shaping the pouch from the ileum.
The surgeon performs a leak test on the ileal pouch before creating the anastomosis, with the test result showing no leakage.
The surgeon places a persisting stick with 2-0 Prolene suture and inserts an EEA-XXVIII anvil into the ileal pouch.
Under laparoscopic view, the surgeon creates an intracorporeal anastomosis and performs a poshoscopy, observing the surgical site for any abnormalities.
A loop of bowel proximal to the ileal pouch is identified and exteriorized through the surgical incision.
The surgeon completes the maturation of the ileostomy, finalizing the creation of the stoma on the abdominal surface.
The surgeon displays the intact specimen, likely the bowel segment or pouch, following the completion of the anastomosis and leak test.
The patient's postoperative recovery was uneventful.
This case demonstrates the feasibility and safety of single-port surgery in IPA patients using the left lower quadrant ileostomacite.
